scene_title: Elsinore. A room in the castle

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: I will not speak with her.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: She is importunate, indeed distract:

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Her mood will needs be pitied.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: What would she have?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: She speaks much of her father; says she hears

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: There’s tricks i’ the world; and hems, and beats her heart;

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Spurns enviously at straws; speaks things in doubt,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: That carry but half sense: her speech is nothing,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Yet the unshaped use of it doth move

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The hearers to collection; they aim at it,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: And botch the words up fit to their own thoughts;

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Which, as her winks, and nods, and gestures yield them,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Indeed would make one think there might be thought,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Though nothing sure, yet much unhappily.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: ’Twere good she were spoken with; for she may strew

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Dangerous conjectures in ill-breeding minds.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Let her come in.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: To my sick soul, as sin’s true nature is,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Each toy seems prologue to some great amiss:

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: So full of artless jealousy is guilt,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: It spills itself in fearing to be spilt.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Where is the beauteous majesty of Denmark?

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: How now, Ophelia!

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: How should I your true love know From another one? By his

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: cockle hat and staff, And his sandal shoon.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Alas, sweet lady, what imports this song?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Say you? nay, pray you, mark. He is dead and gone, lady,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: He is dead and gone; At his head a grass-green turf, At

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: his heels a stone.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Nay, but, Ophelia—

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Pray you, mark. White his shroud as the mountain snow—

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Alas, look here, my lord.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Larded with sweet flowers; Which bewept to the grave did go With true-love showers.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: How do you, pretty lady?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Well, God ’ild you! They say the owl was a baker’s daughter.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Lord, we know what we are, but know not what we may

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: be. God be at your table!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Conceit upon her father.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Pray you, let’s have no words of this; but when they ask

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: you what it means, say you this: To-morrow is Saint Valentine’s day,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: All in the morning betime, And I a maid at your window,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: To be your Valentine. Then up he rose, and donn’d his clothes,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And dupp’d the chamber-door; Let in the maid, that out a maid

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Never departed more.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Pretty Ophelia!

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Indeed, la, without an oath, I’ll make an end on’t: By Gis

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: and by Saint Charity, Alack, and fie for shame! Young men will

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: do’t, if they come to’t; By cock, they are to blame. Quoth

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: she, before you tumbled me, You promised me to wed. So would

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: I ha’ done, by yonder sun, An thou hadst not come to

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: my bed.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: How long hath she been thus?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: I hope all will be well. We must be patient: but I

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: cannot choose but weep, to think they should lay him i’ the

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: cold ground. My brother shall know of it: and so I thank

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: you for your good counsel. Come, my coach! Good night, ladies; good

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: night, sweet ladies; good night, good night.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Follow her close; give her good watch, I pray you.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O, this is the poison of deep grief; it springs

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: All from her father’s death. O Gertrude, Gertrude,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: When sorrows come, they come not single spies,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But in battalions. First, her father slain:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Next, your son gone; and he most violent author

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Of his own just remove: the people muddied,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Thick and unwholesome in their thoughts and whispers,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: For good Polonius’ death; and we have done but greenly,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In hugger-mugger to inter him: poor Ophelia

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Divided from herself and her fair judgment,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Without the which we are pictures, or mere beasts:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Last, and as much containing as all these,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Her brother is in secret come from France;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Feeds on his wonder, keeps himself in clouds,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And wants not buzzers to infect his ear

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: With pestilent speeches of his father’s death;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Wherein necessity, of matter beggar’d,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Will nothing stick our person to arraign

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In ear and ear. O my dear Gertrude, this,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Like to a murdering-piece, in many places

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Gives me superfluous death. A noise within.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Alack, what noise is this?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Where are my Switzers? Let them guard the door.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Save yourself, my lord:

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The ocean, overpeering of his list,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Eats not the flats with more impetuous haste

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Than young Laertes, in a riotous head,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: O’erbears your officers. The rabble call him lord;

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: And, as the world were now but to begin,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Antiquity forgot, custom not known,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The ratifiers and props of every word,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: They cry “Choose we: Laertes shall be king:”

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Caps, hands, and tongues, applaud it to the clouds:

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: “Laertes shall be king, Laertes king!”

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: How cheerfully on the false trail they cry!

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: O, this is counter, you false Danish dogs!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The doors are broke. Noise within.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Where is this king? Sirs, stand you all without.

lines.speaker: DANES
lines.text: No, let’s come in.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I pray you, give me leave.

lines.speaker: DANES
lines.text: We will, we will. They retire without the door.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I thank you: keep the door. O thou vile king,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Give me my father!

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Calmly, good Laertes.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: That drop of blood that’s calm proclaims me bastard,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Cries cuckold to my father, brands the harlot

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Even here, between the chaste unsmirched brow

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Of my true mother.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: What is the cause, Laertes,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That thy rebellion looks so giant-like?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Let him go, Gertrude; do not fear our person:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: There’s such divinity doth hedge a king,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That treason can but peep to what it would,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Acts little of his will. Tell me, Laertes,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Why thou art thus incensed. Let him go, Gertrude.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Speak, man.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Where is my father?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Dead.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: But not by him.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Let him demand his fill.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: How came he dead? I’ll not be juggled with:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: To hell, allegiance! vows, to the blackest devil!

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Conscience and grace, to the profoundest pit!

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I dare damnation. To this point I stand,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: That both the worlds I give to negligence,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Let come what comes; only I’ll be revenged

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Most thoroughly for my father.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Who shall stay you?

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: My will, not all the world:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And for my means, I’ll husband them so well,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: They shall go far with little.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Good Laertes,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: If you desire to know the certainty

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Of your dear father’s death, is’t writ in your revenge,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That, swoopstake, you will draw both friend and foe,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Winner and loser?

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: None but his enemies.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Will you know them then?

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: To his good friends thus wide I’ll ope my arms;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And like the kind life-rendering pelican,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Repast them with my blood.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Why, now you speak

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Like a good child and a true gentleman.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That I am guiltless of your father’s death,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And am most sensible in grief for it,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: It shall as level to your judgment pierce

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: As day does to your eye.

lines.speaker: DANES
lines.text: Let her come in.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: How now! what noise is that?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: They bore him barefaced on the bier; Hey non nonny, nonny, hey

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: nonny; And in his grave rain’d many a tear:— Fare you well,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: my dove!

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Hadst thou thy wits, and didst persuade revenge,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: It could not move thus.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: You must sing a-down a-down, An you call him a-down-a. O, how

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: the wheel becomes it! It is the false steward, that stole his

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: master’s daughter.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: This nothing’s more than matter.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: There’s rosemary, that’s for remembrance; pray, love, remember: and there is pansies, that’s for thoughts.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: A document in madness, thoughts and remembrance fitted.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: There’s fennel for you, and columbines: there’s rue for you; and here’s

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: some for me: we may call it herb-grace o’ Sundays: O you

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: must wear your rue with a difference. There’s a daisy: I would

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: give you some violets, but they withered all when my father died:

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: they say he made a good end— For bonny sweet Robin is

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: all my joy.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Thought and affliction, passion, hell itself,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: She turns to favour and to prettiness.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And will he not come again? And will he not come again?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: No, no, he is dead: Go to thy death-bed: He never will

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: come again. His beard was as white as snow, All flaxen was

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: his poll: He is gone, he is gone, And we cast away

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: moan: God ha’ mercy on his soul! And of all Christian souls,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: I pray God. God be wi’ ye.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Do you see this, O God?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Laertes, I must commune with your grief,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Or you deny me right. Go but apart,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Make choice of whom your wisest friends you will,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And they shall hear and judge ’twixt you and me:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: If by direct or by collateral hand

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: They find us touch’d, we will our kingdom give,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Our crown, our life, and all that we can ours,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To you in satisfaction; but if not,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Be you content to lend your patience to us,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And we shall jointly labour with your soul

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To give it due content.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Let this be so;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: His means of death, his obscure funeral—

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: No trophy, sword, nor hatchment o’er his bones,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: No noble rite nor formal ostentation—

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Cry to be heard, as ’twere from heaven to earth,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: That I must call’t in question.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: So you shall;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And where the offence is let the great axe fall.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I pray you, go with me.

stage_directions.text: Enter Queen, Horatio, and a Gentleman.

O heat, dry up my brains! tears seven times salt, Burn out the sense and virtue of mine eye! By heaven, thy madness shall be paid by weight, Till our scale turn the beam. O rose of May! Dear maid, kind sister, sweet Ophelia! O heavens! is’t possible, a young maid’s wits Should be as moral as an old man’s life? Nature is fine in love, and where ’tis fine, It sends some precious instance of itself After the thing it loves.

glosses.definition: sometimes bawdy slang for female genitals (Elizabethan pun)
a grassy mound at his head

glosses.phrase: the owl was a baker’s daughter
glosses.definition: folktale: a baker's daughter was turned into an owl for stinginess to Jesus

annotations.title: Ophelia's songs: the breaking of a voice
annotations.body: Ophelia's mad scenes substitute singing for speech, and the songs are not madness but scraps of a coherent emotional truth. The pilgrim song ('How should I your true love know / From another one?'), the Valentine's Day song with its sexual frankness ('Young men will do't, if they come to't; / By Cock, they are to blame'), and the song of the dead father ('He is gone, he is gone') articulate, through the displacement of received lyric, what Ophelia cannot say directly. Showalter's influential 'Representing Ophelia' argues that feminist criticism must recover the scene's meaning from the long tradition that aestheticized her madness as picturesque pathos. The songs are sharp instruments of accusation.

annotations.title: The flowers and the language of herbs
annotations.body: Ophelia distributes flowers by their traditional meanings: rosemary for remembrance, pansies for thoughts, fennel for flattery, columbines for marital infidelity, rue for sorrow and repentance, daisy for dissembled love, violets (withered) for faithfulness. Elizabethan herbals, particularly John Gerard's Herball (1597), catalogued these significations and audiences would have read the tableau in real time. Ophelia's distributions are targeted: fennel and columbines (flattery and infidelity) to the King and Queen; rue to Gertrude; the withered violets since 'they all withered when my father died.' The scene is Ophelia's most articulate political speech, disguised as a flower game.

annotations.title: Laertes's insurrection and Hamlet's absent parallel
annotations.body: Laertes storms the castle with an armed mob shouting 'Laertes shall be king,' threatening Claudius with immediate deposition. The contrast with Hamlet is structural: given the same provocation (a father murdered), Laertes acts at once and politically, raising popular support and confronting the sovereign. Shakespeare deliberately places Laertes's vengeance beside Hamlet's to force comparison — but the comparison does not favor either man. Laertes will let Claudius turn his revenge into the King's own instrument within twenty lines. The play keeps showing us characters who act decisively and are destroyed by their decisiveness, and characters who delay and are destroyed by their delay. There is no correct speed at which to avenge a father.

annotations.title: 'There's rosemary, that's for remembrance'
annotations.body: The most quoted of Ophelia's flower lines is also the most theatrically charged: she gives rosemary to someone — the stage direction in modern editions is ambiguous, and Laertes's 'A document in madness' suggests he recognizes its pointedness. Rosemary was associated with both weddings and funerals, and the double resonance fits Ophelia's condition — the bride-never-to-be who is already mourning. 'Pray, love, remember' is an instruction to the living to honor the dead, and in performance its recipient matters: Gertrude? Laertes? The King? Ophelia gives herself no rosemary, which the tradition would have noted: she is asking to be the one whose own loss is carried forward by others.

trials.prompt: What condition does Ophelia demonstrate in her first appearance here?
options: She is composed and demands justice for her father.
She is mad, singing fragments of old songs about lost love and dead fathers.
She is weeping silently and refuses to speak.
She has joined Laertes's armed uprising.

trials.prompt: What does the Valentine's Day song suggest about Ophelia's distress?
options: That she has taken up a new religion.
That sexual betrayal (by a lover who promised marriage then refused) is among the griefs driving her madness.
That she misses her time at court festivals.
That Polonius promised her marriage to a stranger.

trials.prompt: What does Laertes do when he returns to Denmark?
options: He presents himself humbly to Claudius for counsel.
He bursts in with a mob of followers shouting 'Laertes shall be king,' ready to kill the King.
He searches the castle for Polonius's body.
He challenges Hamlet to a duel by proxy.

trials.prompt: Which flower does Ophelia associate with 'thoughts'?
options: Rosemary
Pansies
Fennel
Rue

trials.prompt: What does the contrast between Laertes's immediate revenge and Hamlet's delay suggest?
options: That Laertes is the morally superior brother.
That Shakespeare is demonstrating the correct way to avenge a father.
That neither speed nor delay is safe — both revengers will be destroyed.
That Hamlet's philosophy has paralyzed him while Laertes acts freely.

trials.prompt: Why are the violets Ophelia mentions 'withered'?
options: The season is late in summer and they have died.
They withered when her father died — faithfulness has died with Polonius.
A servant has neglected to water them.
She is referring to a plant disease in Denmark.
